so supplementing him with process, tools, people to execute great content would be amazing.
Another compelling thing is that we're really trying to hone in on owning direct materials, which is a specific area that our business focuses on.
And just to give you some context, George, we're procurement software.
And there's two types of procurement.
There's direct and there's indirect.
Direct procurement is anything a company buys to put into its product.
So when I was a Tesla, direct procurement would be Tesla buying car parts, right?
That would be like steering wheels and windshields, you know, that kind of thing.
Now Tesla also buys, for reference, paper and urinals.
And janitorial stuff, and office chairs and desks, right?
Like, that's still corporate spend, but it's indirect.
And so there's tons of solutions out there to help you buy stuff.
Amazon Business, Ramp and Brex, Coupa, Concur, like all Zip, HQ is one that's been growing a lot recently.
So that's what they're focused on.
But there's almost nothing out there except for LightSource for helping the procurement teams actually buy their direct materials.
It's bad, you know?
And so these are a few things that I'm rethinking about.
And, you know, really owning the direct material space.

And we have a new category that we're trying to build up called spec to scale, which is, you know, in the indirect space, there's something called S2P, source to pay, which breaks down into source to contract, like finding a supplier, sourcing.

So, managing revisions of parts, figuring out which quote corresponds to which revision from suppliers, allowing unlimited flexibility in the data that you can collect, and then seeing how all of that rolls up to the ultimate final cost of the thing that they're purchasing, and helping them sift through all the data that comes through this process.

Savings ideas and opportunities, supplier portal for their responses.
So I think they would say like, it kind of helps us manage like a lot of different things, not just one small slice of the puzzle.

very specific to direct materials in a way.

We don't have customer success managers as much as we have something called deployment strategists.

And so we actually have a range of these ex-McKinsey folks on our team that will actually meet with the customer, help them implement the software, help them set up their sourcing pipeline, and then be like an ongoing resource.

can either, people will either change their tools or their process, but not both at once.
And so we've built a system that allows you to bring your own quote documents to the platform, and then it can be structured in the platform with no code.
And so you don't have to conform to whatever our templates are, you can bring your own.
go.

So the platform is designed to do all this stuff.

I think.

Like, a place where people can go, not just to learn about, like, LightSource and our quirky insights, but to just be, like, okay, like, I'm going to build up a 101 foundational understanding of procurement, and, like, I want to go here to, to sort of get it better.
Um, and I think, like, another nice to have, I would say, is, like, if the content generally is helpful in accelerating our time to close, so, you know, there is a sale.
And I think that you can, like, lubricate that pipeline by having great content.
So when the IT person who generally doesn't want to buy LightSource comes to our site, they don't say, like, wait, we already have SAP at home.
Why do you need restaurant-bought, you know, LightSource?
But they instead say, okay, cool, there's things about this that are actually beyond the capability that we have today.
Yeah, I think there's actually specifically the point that we work well with organizations that already have something like SAP and Oracle set up.
I think that's something that oftentimes really allays concerns with the IT people.

Yeah, we've typically seen, like, especially if we get into that geolift, like, people that are searching and researching within those LLMs, they tend to have, you know, like a 3 to 4x higher conversion rate because they've already.
So I think as we continue to kind of do some of the SEO and Geo work of trying to figure out where your audience, what are they searching for?
How do we show up in that conversation?
It would be interesting to see if that kind of helps with that sales velocity as well.
Yeah, I think it's pretty applicable here, maybe just for me to speak to a little bit about how we think about the first couple weeks and then going to week eight in terms of SEO and reach.
And so for the clients that it's worked best for, the most collaborative and the most open to responding to early signals.
And so everything we do at its root is audience first.
Our content is audience first.
We want to speak to the customer in the moments of their purchase from top of funnel all the way to the bottom of funnel.
And, you know, that's why we'll dive super deep to get to your audience.
But what we've seen in the first like one to four...
Weeks is we're solving for reach.
So it's what content can we create and signals can we track to see how this is resonating.
And so volume works better there.
We're essentially testing your audience with content to see what's working, what's resonating.
Each week we double down on what's working both on the AEO and the SEO front.
And then it's after you kind of solve for reach, like what's getting indexed, what's getting impressions, now what's getting clicks to the website.
Then we're seeing with the clients who are more bought in early on with that similar mindset.

Then week seven and eight, we can start beginning to track, you know, those conversion metrics and what's moving in the audience only because, you know, we're so focused on the early signals and the what's working now versus just a massively broad approach.
And just kind of throwing things at the wall.
We, we, we have seen that being more surgical with our strategy.
And with our response to what's working, has worked better for our clients.
Makes sense.
All right.
How would you guys describe your ideal customer today?
Any archetypes or personas or anything in that arena?
And generally, the ICP, from an account standpoint, are enterprise manufacturers.
So Global 2000, Fortune 500, manufacturing a complex product and leveraging direct materials to do so.
So generally, we're measuring that based on the size of their team that we can find online.
Looking at their 10K, seeing their cogs.
Are they publicly, privately traded?
Are they recent funding?
Have they announced any, you know, new products?
Or are they a new innovation lab within a legacy midfactor?
Usually they'll have, like, an engineering team or a design team that's working closely with procurement.
And then we can also see that they have, like, a PLM and an ERP and or an ERP.
Usually a PLM at least.
We know that they're manufacturing something complex enough to require procurement.
I think that also organizations that are rapidly iterating and launching new products tend to be really good for us.
Companies that are growing quickly tend to be just, in general, more apt to buying software.
Are you interested in like the personas within the ICP company, or are you just interested in the company type?
Both.
Okay, so we can go a little bit deeper on this.
So in terms of like industries, like the industries that we serve most, most directly are automotive, aerospace and defense.
So this would be like screen manufacturing, industrial, yeah, aerospace and defense, industrial, you can say industrial chemicals, consumer electronics.
Medical devices, we have a bunch of med device customers, like Dan and Her and Medtronic.
What else?
Industrial food, so like folks like Yum Brands, KFC, Taco Bell, Pizza Hut.
They use LightSource not to source like car parts, but to source chicken.
Then within the, and then there's, you know, pretty much anything that's in, like, an adjacent category.
If they're doing discrete or process manufacturing, they could be a LightSource customer.
But I think we don't really work with, like, if they don't have a physical product, like, we're probably not the right software for them.
Gotcha.
Then in terms of, like, the personas within the ICP that we work with, there's sort of, I would say there's, like, or four verticals.
The first, our core user group, is the procurement organization.
So this would be titles, like, from leadership down, chief procurement officer, VP of procurement, director of procurement, and then, like, you might have.
Like category managers, unfortunately, there's a range of different names that people will use.
You have category managers, can have buyers, you can have sourcing managers, you might have global supply managers, global commodity managers, GCMs.
So every organization calls them a different thing, but they're basically the people who work with suppliers.
The other orgs that are usually helpful are like the supply chain org.
So supply chain is typically a little bit different from procurement.
Sometimes procurement rolls up to supply chain, sometimes not.
But supply chain would be like a chief supply chain officer, VP of supply chain, that kind of thing.
Other groups that are useful, IT.
And we see IT in sort of two flavors.
I would say centralized IT or federated IT.
Centralized IT is like chief information officer.
Um...
It's like an IT org that serves the whole business.
And then what I would say is like federated IT would be IT that lives within the business units.
And so you might have like procurement IT or supply chain IT.

And those groups tend to really like us and we work really well with them because they're often like looking for the best solution for their area versus just like, hey, we have SAP and that's that, you know.
Other users, there's two other user groups that I think are worth being aware of, engineering, like design engineering.
So these would be people who are the engineers developing the products.
They will use LightSource to send it out to suppliers.
And then you also have finance teams, specifically if they're like project finance or like strategic finance.
And so these are folks who are on the finance team or focused on things like tracking the total bill of materials.
They sit within finance, but they're doing a lot less like accounting FP&A and doing a lot more in terms of managing of a given program.
So they're not doing really accounting or FP&A.
That's not their focus.
So, like, we don't really work with these teams.
Accounting teams, I think finance CFOs at a general level might like us, but they're not really close enough to what we do to have a real opinion.
But the finance teams that work on managing bills and material often are a very good stakeholder for us.
And then the final group is quality teams, supplier quality.

And these folks, for reference, are, they, like, do supplier industrialization, like, going to the supplier's location, making sure the supplier is ready for production, checking parts to make sure that the parts are on the spec, all sorts of.
Things like that, and they'll use LightSource for something called PPAP, Production Part Approval Process.
Yeah, PPAP, P-P-A-P, it's all one word.
Yeah, uppercase P-P-A-P.
Yeah, that's pretty much it.
Just a few.
And then the final stakeholder actually are suppliers.
So the suppliers are, they don't really choose to use LightSource.
LightSource, their customer asks them to respond in LightSource.
And so they're like sort of a stakeholder, but they're not really like a core, they are a core user, but they're not the decision maker on the purchase.
And it's typically at the suppliers, there's like two or three personas within the supplier.
One is the account manager, who's like the sales or the sales guy, the account executive, account executive or account manager, who manages relationship with the procurement person.
The second might be someone who focuses

This is on quoting, and so they have a lot of different titles for this, but people who are doing, like, quote response or quote construction, I don't even really know what their titles are, but they're, like, helping people with the RFQ process, responding to requests for quote, and then sometimes the supplier has also an engineering team that needs to get looped in.

The supplier to figure out, like, hey, how much is it going to cost us to make this stamping, they might have to include the engineer to be, like, what's the tonnage of the press that's going to be used?

I think that one situation that we've seen come up is if the organization has had some sort of issues in the past delivering their product, or they're ramping up a new program, new product, and they need, like, they see that they need a tool in order to orchestrate the procurement process to make sure that they're delivering on time.
So those are two common triggers that we've seen.

Like, for example, in the automotive realm, it might be introducing a new model of vehicle, is the new product.

I think another trigger is when they have a new procurement leadership in place.
That, I think, is often an opening, because they will want to change certain things up.

Another one is if they get acquired by a private equity firm, because when a company is acquired by a private equity firm, they typically want to squeeze out a lot of costs, because it makes the company more profitable, which helps the private equity firm sell them for a markup.
And so private equity-owned businesses tend to be very hawkish on, like, finding savings.
I think other positive signals are when...
when...

A company engages with a consulting firm, because typically when they're engaging with a consulting firm, it's because the business is somehow in trouble and needs to reorient something about its structure or cost basis.
And often there will be a digital transformation component of those projects, and so it gives a nice opening for us as well.
I think when their existing sourcing tool comes up for renewal, that's also a good opening for us.

I think it's a really good question.
I think the times where they don't go with LightSource, it's either there's maybe three or four failure modes.
One is they feel that they can build something internally that is sufficiently good, and they feel that that will be cheaper and easier for them to stand up and do the change management on.
The second is if they decide to go with or proceed with an end-to-end suite, like an SAP or Oracle implementation, that will often have a procurement resourcing tool, albeit it's an underpowered one.
And then their procurement team, their IT team might just be like, hey, isn't this good enough for now?
Sometimes we lose to other...
Other sourcing tools, like we'll sometimes use to Archlit or Kielvar, those are other sort of point solutions for sourcing.
They're more focused on indirect, but sometimes people don't need very much on the indirect side to be happy, and so they might go with one of those tools.
I think Archlit's ease of use is generally pretty high, and so we come up against, and they're sort of a new shiny object also like we are, and so they come up a good amount.
Then the other thing is the do nothing.

I think do nothing happens all the time, where people get to a place where they're like seeing value, but they're not over the hump of inertia to actually start using the platform or do something with it.
We've lost on price a few times, but I usually think that's not because of price.
I usually think it's because we haven't established enough value in the sales process.
Sometimes we'll lose RFPs, like...
The RFP is a, we try not to compete with them if we can avoid it, but if we didn't write the RFP, we're typically not going to win it.
RFPs are like checklists, and so the person that wins the RFP is not the person who has the best solution, it's the person who has the most features.
Because if you have the most features, then you can check off the most boxes.
And so you might look at LightSource and say, oh, it's got 70 out of 100 features.
When I look at GEP, it's got 99 out of 100 features.
Now, that's just because they can do everything, they've just built little widgets for it, but like it doesn't measure like what degree the feature is covered well, right?
It's like saying, it's like someone saying like, you know, Hotmail is the same as Gmail, you know, it's just a different name.
It's like, no, there might be things about Gmail that are actually better, you know, than Hotmail, or AOL, I guess.
